questions.prompt: If matrix $A$ has 2 rows and 3 columns, and matrix $B$ has 3 rows and 4 columns, what is the size of the product $AB$?

llmContext.overviewText: Matrix multiplication $AB$ is defined when the number of columns of $A$ equals the number of rows of $B$. The result has the same number of rows as $A$ and the same number of columns as $B$.

nodes.title: Check compatibility
nodes.reference: For $AB$, we need (columns of $A$) = (rows of $B$). Here $A$ is $2\times 3$ and $B$ is $3\times 4$, so 3 = 3 works.

nodes.title: Size of the product
nodes.reference: $AB$ has rows = rows of $A$ and columns = columns of $B$. So $AB$ is $2\times 4$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
Compute the matrix-vector product:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}.$$

What is the *first* entry of the result?

llmContext.overviewText: Multiply the matrix by the column vector: first row dot vector gives first entry, second row dot vector gives second entry.

nodes.title: First entry
nodes.reference: First row of the matrix is $[1, 2]$. Dot with $[2, 1]$ gives $1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 1 = 2 + 2 = 4$.

nodes.title: Second entry (if needed)
nodes.reference: Second row $[3, 4]$ dot $[2, 1]$ gives $3\cdot 2 + 4\cdot 1 = 10$. So the result is $[4, 10]^{\top}$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
Solve for $x$ and $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + y &= 5 \\ x - y &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

What is the value of $x$?

llmContext.overviewText: Two linear equations in two unknowns. Add or subtract equations to eliminate one variable, or use substitution.

nodes.title: Eliminate $y$
nodes.reference: Adding the two equations gives $2x+y + x-y = 5+1$, so $3x = 6$, hence $x = 2$.

nodes.title: Find $y$
nodes.reference: Substitute $x=2$ into $x-y=1$ to get $2-y=1$, so $y=1$. Check: $2(2)+1=5$ and $2-1=1$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt: What is the dot product of $\mathbf{u} = (3, 4)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (2, -1)$?

llmContext.overviewText: The dot product of two vectors is the sum of the products of their corresponding entries: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = u_1 v_1 + u_2 v_2$.
llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
Find the determinant of

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}.$$

Use the formula $\det(A) = ad - bc$ for a $2\times 2$ matrix.

llmContext.overviewText: For a $2\times 2$ matrix with entries $a,b$ in the first row and $c,d$ in the second row, $\det = ad - bc$.
llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a *linear* equation in $x$ and $y$? (A linear equation has no products like $xy$, no powers like $x^2$, and variables only to the first power.)

llmContext.overviewText: A linear equation in $x$ and $y$ can be written as $ax + by = c$ for constants $a$, $b$, $c$. No $x^2$, $y^2$, $xy$, or functions like $1/x$.

nodes.title: What makes it linear
nodes.reference: Linear means each variable appears only to the first power and is multiplied by a constant. $2x+3y=6$ fits. The others have $x^2$, $xy$, or $1/x$.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

llmContext.overviewText: Scalar multiplication: $c\mathbf{v}$ multiplies each entry by $c$. Vector addition: add corresponding entries.
llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt: Two lines in the plane have equations $y = 2x + 1$ and $y = 2x - 3$. How many points $(x,y)$ satisfy *both* equations?

llmContext.overviewText: Both lines have the same slope (2) but different $y$-intercepts (1 and $-3$). So they are parallel and never intersect.

nodes.title: Compare slopes
nodes.reference: Same slope means parallel lines. Different intercepts mean they are distinct, so they do not cross. The system has no solution.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt: A snack stand sells drinks for **2 dollars** each and pretzels for **3 dollars** each. Sarah buys 2 drinks and 1 pretzel. Which expression gives her total cost?

llmContext.overviewText: Total cost = (price per drink $\times$ number of drinks) + (price per pretzel $\times$ number of pretzels). This is a linear combination.

nodes.title: Compute cost
nodes.reference: 2 drinks at 2 dollars each: $2\cdot 2$. 1 pretzel at 3 dollars: $3\cdot 1$. Total = $2(2)+3(1) = 4+3 = 7$ dollars.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.

questions.prompt:
Consider the system

$$\begin{aligned} x + y &= 3 \\ 2x + 2y &= 6 \end{aligned}$$

The second equation is just 2 times the first. How many solutions $(x,y)$ does this system have?

llmContext.overviewText: When one equation is a multiple of the other, they represent the same line. Every point on that line is a solution.

nodes.title: Same line
nodes.reference: $2x+2y=6$ is $2(x+y)=2(3)$, so it is the same condition as $x+y=3$. So we only have one line; any point on it (e.g. $(0,3)$, $(1,2)$, $(3,0)$) is a solution.

llmContext.note: This is reference guidance only. It is not a strict rubric.
